Q1: I'm pretty disciplined about my study routine, honestly. I typically dedicate about 30 hours a week to studying, which I spread across most days. I prefer reading and writing things out, so I spend a lot of time with printed notes and handwritten summaries. I usually study in the library because I don't have great resources at home, and it helps me stay focused. My routine is structured around making sure I deeply understand the material rather than just skimming through it.
Q2: I try to keep things regular, but I'd be lying if I said deadlines didn't shift things around sometimes. Generally, I have set times each day when I know I'll be studying, usually mornings and late afternoons. When big assignments come up, I might add extra evening sessions, but I try not to let it get chaotic. Consistency helps me manage stress, which is something I've worked hard to keep low.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check announcements or see if any new materials were posted. Sometimes it was to look at discussion prompts or see what my peers were saying. I didn't log in just to browse around—I had specific purposes each time. With limited internet access at home, I had to be intentional about when I used the LMS.
Q4: It became routine early on when I realized I needed to check for updates regularly. I'd log in maybe six times a week, usually at the library where the wifi is reliable. It wasn't something I enjoyed doing, but it became a necessary habit. Once I established that pattern, it just felt like part of my weekly schedule, like checking email.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me because that's how I learn best. I appreciated when lecturers posted detailed notes or PDFs I could download and annotate. The discussion boards were also helpful because I could see different perspectives and clarify my understanding through writing responses. I contributed quite a bit to discussions because articulating ideas helps me process them.
Q6: I didn't watch many videos—only about 20 minutes total, I think. Videos just don't work well for my learning style; I find myself zoning out and needing to rewind constantly. I'd rather read a transcript or summary. I also ignored some of the optional interactive elements because they felt like they were designed for a different type of learner.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it helped me understand concepts more deeply or prepare for exams. I'm very focused on mastering the material, so anything that contributed to that goal was valuable. If something felt like busywork or didn't connect to the core content, I'd deprioritize it. I also valued activities where I could engage with peers because teaching or explaining things reinforces my own learning.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they felt like extras I could skip if I was pressed for time. Required activities got done no matter what because they affected my grade directly. With optional stuff, I'd weigh whether the time investment would actually help me learn better. Sometimes I did them, especially discussion posts, but other times I let them go.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, then print them out at the library to study from paper. I also used my own handwritten notes extensively—rewriting and summarizing is how I really learn. I'd sometimes use textbooks from the library too, older editions that were available for free. The LMS was more of a starting point than my main study environment.
Q10: There were many times when I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like when I spent hours reviewing my printed notes and rewriting summaries by hand. Or when I was discussing concepts with classmates in person at the library. Those study sessions were some of my most productive, but they left no digital trace at all.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach. I'd download everything I needed in one session, then work from those files offline for days. With limited internet access, it made sense to batch my downloads. I'd only go back to the LMS when I needed something new or wanted to check discussions. My offline study time far exceeded my online time.
Q12: There were moments when I felt sad and it was harder to motivate myself, even though I generally handle stress well. Sometimes the weight of managing everything with limited resources got to me. The CBT sessions I attended helped me work through those feelings and get back on track. I'm resilient, so these dips never lasted long, but they were real.
Q13: Fatigue definitely affected me sometimes, especially when I'd been putting in long hours. When I was tired, I might log into the LMS but not really absorb what I was looking at. Competing responsibilities, like needing to work or help family, occasionally pulled my attention away. But I'd always circle back and make sure I caught up on what mattered.
Q14: Yes, there were times I opened materials and just couldn't focus. My eyes would scan the page but nothing would stick. Usually this happened when I was mentally exhausted or preoccupied with something else. I learned to recognize when this was happening and would step away rather than waste time pretending to study.
Q15: Sometimes I'd postpone starting on assignments even when I knew they were important. It wasn't laziness—it was more about needing to feel mentally ready to do quality work. I'd rather wait until I could give something my full attention than rush through it poorly. My assignment scores weren't as high as my exam scores, partly because of this.
Q16: Deadlines definitely shaped when I engaged with the LMS more intensively. I'd check in more frequently as due dates approached to make sure I wasn't missing anything. But I tried not to be purely deadline-driven—I preferred steady progress over cramming. Still, deadlines created urgency that sometimes pushed me to log in more than usual.
Q17: Feedback motivated me to refine my understanding and approach. When I received comments on assignments, I'd really think about them and adjust my studying accordingly. Positive feedback reinforced that my methods were working, while constructive criticism helped me identify gaps. I appreciated detailed feedback more than just grades.
Q18: The course structure gave me clear milestones to work toward, which helped me plan my time. Having a predictable schedule of when materials would be released made it easier to organize my offline study. I appreciated when the structure was logical and built on previous content. It let me create my own study system around it.
Q19: The availability of downloadable materials made engagement much easier for someone like me with limited internet access. What made it harder was when important information was only in video format—I'd have to find workarounds. Also, some interactive elements required being online, which wasn't always feasible. Overall though, the design accommodated different learning styles reasonably well.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for information or trying to understand what's expected without actually completing work. They could be anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. Or maybe they're struggling to translate their engagement into finished products. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: This definitely reflects my experience. I performed very well on exams despite relatively low LMS activity because my real learning happened offline. I'd download what I needed and then study intensively from printed materials and handwritten notes. The LMS was a tool for accessing content, not where my actual learning took place. My distinction grade came from deep offline engagement.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and submission timing pretty accurately. They can track what resources were opened and basic participation in discussions. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether someone actually understood what they read. They completely miss offline study, peer discussions, and the mental work of processing information.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand and master the material, not just going through the motions. It's about being mentally present and curious, asking questions, and connecting new information to what you already know. For me, engagement looks like spending hours with my notes, participating thoughtfully in discussions, and really caring about learning. It's an internal state that doesn't always show externally.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent with printed materials, rewriting notes, or discussing concepts with classmates face-to-face. It wouldn't capture the emotional journey—the moments of sadness I worked through or the satisfaction of finally understanding something difficult. It also wouldn't show my resilience, how I bounced back from hard days. My real engagement story is mostly invisible to the system.
